upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a remarkably stable atmosphere with a near-isothermal layer from the surface to 850mb — the lapse rate of only 0.26°C/km (vs. dry adiabatic 9.8°C/km) indicates a very strong low-level inversion capping convection near 1500m MSL. Above 850mb, temperatures drop more normally but the cap is effective at limiting thermal development. Winds are light and variable through the column (2kt at surface, 10kt from S at 850mb, backing to 140° at 700mb), suggesting minimal wind shear but also no strong thermal trigger aloft.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. METARs are benign: KRHV showing light S winds 6-10kt with gusts to 14kt and SCT090 — scattered clouds at 9,000ft. KHAF clearing nicely with NW winds 6-10kt, 10SM visibility, and CLR skies — no marine layer present at coastal reference station. KSFO calm (5kt NE), KAPC warm at 27°C with light E wind (gradient reversal typical of post-frontal), KWVI also warm at 28°C with light SW. The KAPC/KWVI warmth relative to coast signals afternoon sea breeze potential.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, W/NW-facing high launch
sites.howItWorks: The 1750ft launch is a thermal site that also catches the afternoon W/NW sea breeze as it funnels through the Diablo Range gaps. Ideal conditions are W-NW 8-15kt with thermals to at least 2000ft AGL. Morning thermals trigger off the sunlit ridge when the marine layer clears; afternoon sea breeze provides a reliable pressure gradient.
sites.todaySetup: Today's main challenge is the persistent cloud cover capping thermal development through most of the day. The BL height starts at just 200m at 8am and while it climbs impressively to ~1790m by 4pm, cloud cover is at 99% at dawn, clears partially midday (55% at noon, only 18% at 1pm), then clamps back to 100% overcast by 2-5pm — killing the thermal window just as the BL peaks. Surface winds are very light and southerly in the morning (160-173° at 4-6kt), slowly backing to W/NW in the afternoon (300-351° at 3-5kt), but these are sub-ideal for this launch. The afternoon NW shift arrives after clouds return, so you lose thermals right when the wind direction improves. There is no rain risk, but the combination of morning cloud suppression and afternoon overcast bookending a brief 1-2 hour thermal window around 11am-1pm makes this a frustrating day. Gusts are not a concern — winds stay under 9kt all day at this elevation.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Nearly 100% cloud cover with light S winds 4-6kt — thermals completely suppressed. BL only 200-655m. No flyable thermal or ridge lift.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Clouds partially clear (18-55% coverage around 1pm) and BL climbs to 1290-1555m — a brief thermal window opens. Winds are very light (2-3kt, shifting S to W), making launch timing critical. This is the only viable window of the day, but light and variable winds add challenge.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud cover returns to 100% by 2pm, shutting down thermals despite BL staying elevated. NW winds build to 4-7kt by 5pm — ridge lift marginal. Evening NW ramp-up to 8-9kt at 6-7pm arrives too late and with 91-100% cloud cover.

verdict.hazards: Overcast returns by 2pm killing thermals; light/variable winds make launch commitment risky; brief thermal window sandwiched between two overcast periods

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft launch)
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch is a gentler site suited for P2s practicing thermal and valley breeze flying. It works best with S-SW winds 8-14kt and moderate thermals. Lower elevation means earlier thermal trigger but also more exposure to valley surface winds.
sites.todaySetup: The 600ft launch faces the same overcast problem as the high launch, but with even weaker thermals given the lower elevation and surrounding terrain shading. Morning brings near-calm conditions with S winds 2-3kt and 99% cloud cover — unlandable thermal conditions. By midday the BL climbs to 530-705m and winds back toward SW/W at 4-6kt, offering the best but still modest window. The key issue is that cloud cover at this level fluctuates between 17-70% during the thermal window (noon to 1pm), and by 2pm it's back to 100% overcast. Afternoon NW wind builds to 7-10kt by 5-6pm, which could support light soaring, but 91-100% cloud cover eliminates thermalling. This is a soarable site only if you catch the 12-1pm clearing.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Mostly overcast with light ESE-S winds under 3kt — no thermal or soaring conditions. BL capped below 400m.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: clouds thin to 17-25% around 1pm, BL reaches 700m, SW winds 4-5kt. Marginal but workable thermals possible for experienced pilots. Short window before overcast returns.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast by 2pm eliminates thermals. NW winds build to 10kt by 6pm — possible soaring in ridge flow but cloud base likely at or below ridge. Not recommended.

verdict.hazards: Persistent overcast suppresses thermals; brief clearing window unreliable; afternoon cloud return with building NW wind creates confusion between ridge flow and thermal conditions

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site driven by the prevailing NW sea breeze pushing up the coastal bluff. Ideal conditions are W-NW 10-20kt with no marine layer fog at launch. Wave height and SST from the Half Moon Bay buoy help gauge marine influence and wind consistency.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock has a genuinely interesting day — the data shows a morning period with offshore/cross winds that transitions toward usable W-NW flow by afternoon. Early morning sees SE winds (129°) at 5-8kt — this is the wrong direction for soaring and indicates local drainage or back-pressure. By 10-11am, winds are still from the SE/E (106-123°) which is unflyable for ridge soaring here. The critical shift happens around 1-2pm when the model shows wind backing toward W-SW (261°) then locking in W-NW (254-287°) through the evening at 6-8kt. KHAF METARs confirm this: NW 6-10kt, clear skies, 10SM visibility — the coastal reference is excellent right now. The Half Moon Bay buoy shows SST 14.4°C with modest wave height (0.9m, 9s period), suggesting a shallow but present marine layer that has clearly cleared (KHAF is CLR). Afternoon W-NW flow at 6-8kt is light for ridge soaring — minimum for most pilots is around 10kt. The buoy wind at 9.7kt (SF Bar) suggests adequate gradient. This is a workable afternoon window but on the light side for confident soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: SE to E winds (110-129°) are completely wrong direction for ridge soaring — any attempts would be in rotor. No flyable window. Avoid.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Transition period — winds shift from SE through S toward SW by 1pm (227° at 4kt) then W-SW (261°) by 2pm. Wait for the W/NW lock-in before committing. Cloud cover bounces 65-100%, adding uncertainty.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: W-NW wind establishes at 5-8kt from ~2pm onward, building to 7-8kt by 5-6pm. 100% cloud cover persists (marine influence), but launch is above cloud base if KHAF reports CLR. This is the soaring window — light but workable for P2+ pilots comfortable with the site.

verdict.hazards: Wrong-way winds until ~1-2pm; transition period with variable direction; afternoon flow on light side for confident soaring; 100% overcast but KHAF shows CLR so fog not at ground level

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak relies on thermal development off the sun-heated east bay hills combined with afternoon W/NW sea breeze. Ideal conditions are W-NW 10-15kt with thermals reaching 2000+ ft AGL. It shares the same valley thermal dynamics as Ed Levin but is more exposed to the sea breeze corridor.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the identical cloud problem as Ed Levin — and given nearly identical model data (the sites share the same grid in both GFS and HRRR), the assessment is the same atmospheric story. Morning overcast (99% cloud cover, S winds 4-6kt) completely suppresses thermal development. A partial clearing window opens 11am-1pm when cloud cover drops to 18-55% and BL climbs to 1290-1555m — this is the only viable thermal window. However, surface winds during this window are extremely light (2-3kt) and backing from S toward W, making launch commitment and soaring workability difficult. By 2pm clouds return to 100% and stay there through sunset, despite BL continuing to climb to 1790m — the thermals are capped by cloud rather than the inversion. The afternoon NW wind shift (333-351°) brings 4-7kt by 4-6pm but with zero thermal support under overcast. Mission Peak's higher exposure makes the light-wind thermal window even more demanding than the lower Ed Levin launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 99-81% cloud cover with light S winds 4-5kt — thermals completely blocked. BL only 200-655m. Unlaunchable thermal conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Partial clearing 12-1pm (18-55% cloud) with BL reaching 1290-1555m. Light 2-3kt S-to-W winds create a brief but very light-air thermal window. Experienced pilots only — light and variable near launch requires careful assessment.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast returns by 2pm. NW wind builds to 4-7kt by 4-6pm but no thermal support. Ridge lift marginal given site orientation. Not a viable soaring window.

verdict.hazards: Persistent overcast bookends a very brief midday clearing; winds during the clearing are sub-ideal light/variable; 100% overcast afternoon despite high BL; no punchy conditions but no reliable lift either

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday looks significantly better. Models show clear skies by 10am (only 2% cloud cover at noon), W-NW winds building from 5kt in the morning to 7-9kt by 2-4pm, and BL reaching 305-335m — modest but the clear skies are the key improvement. HRRR confirms this pattern. Thermals will be weak given the low BL, but the combination of clear sky and usable W-NW wind makes for clean soaring. Best window looks like 11am-3pm. P2-friendly if winds stay under 10kt.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same Monday clearing story as the high launch — clear skies by late morning with W-NW winds 9-10kt by afternoon and BL reaching 320-345m. This could be a solid soaring day at the lower launch with steady ridge flow in the 8-10kt range. Tomorrow.io data for Monday shows light gusts (6-8kt), so nothing punchy expected.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Monday shows persistent 100% cloud cover all day with W-NW winds building from 7.5kt in the morning to 12kt by afternoon. This is a stronger ridge soaring day than today — 10-12kt W-NW is squarely in the soaring window. However, 100% overcast all day may mean coastal fog lingering at or near launch. Check KHAF METARs in the morning. If clear at launch, this could be one of the better Mussel Rock days of the week.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Monday mirrors Ed Levin — clear skies developing by late morning, W-NW winds building to 7-9kt by 2-4pm, BL at 305-335m. Very light morning (1-2kt at 8-10am) then a gradual build. Tomorrow.io shows gusts only reaching 8-9kt, so not punchy. A reasonable P2-P3 day with thermals limited by the modest BL but clear conditions.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday (Day 2) maintains the clear pattern with W-NW winds 8-9kt, BL climbing to 895-940m by 2-4pm — a meaningful improvement over Monday. Cloud cover stays below 80% until late afternoon. This could be the best thermal soaring day of the forecast period at Ed Levin, with stronger thermals and usable sea breeze.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday shows similar improvement — W-NW 8-9kt, BL reaching 940m, and mostly clear skies through the prime thermal window. A solid soaring day at both launches.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Tuesday remains overcast (76-100% cloud cover) with W/NW winds in the 8-11kt range — consistent ridge soaring potential if launch is clear of fog. Wave heights from buoy data warrant monitoring for any increase in marine influence.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Tuesday looks like the best Mission Peak day of the forecast — BL reaches 715-850m, winds are W at 4-9kt, and cloud cover stays manageable (50-84%). The 120m wind data shows 9-12kt aloft, suggesting good thermal convection mixing with the sea breeze. P2-P3 day.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Wednesday) data is GFS-only at this range. Pattern shows continued W-NW flow with BL around 695-895m and cloud cover building to 59-78% by afternoon. A reasonable but slightly cloudier day than Tuesday — expect moderate thermals with afternoon cloud suppression.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday appears similar to Tuesday at the 600ft launch — W-NW winds 6-8kt, BL reaching 695-895m, with increasing afternoon cloud cover. Still soarable but the thermal window may be shorter.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Wednesday GFS shows W/NW winds 9-11kt with persistent overcast — similar to Monday/Tuesday pattern. Ridge soaring viable if coastal fog stays below launch. Check morning METARs.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Wednesday GFS shows W winds 4-5kt at surface with strong upper-level flow (11-12kt at 120m), BL reaching 570-850m, and afternoon cloud cover 50-84%. Pattern is similar to Tuesday but slightly lighter surface flow. Moderate thermal day.

watchlist: Today's overcast is the dominant story — the morning cloud deck is suppressing thermals at all inland sites, and it returns by 2pm; the brief 12-1:30pm clearing window is narrow and may not materialize if clouds linger
Mussel Rock wind direction this morning is SE/E (110-130°) — wrong way for ridge soaring; wait for confirmed W-NW shift before driving out, target after 1:30-2pm
KRHV afternoon gusts reached 14kt from S — sea breeze front arrival may punch through faster than expected; monitor for rapid direction shift at Ed Levin
Tomorrow (Monday) Mussel Rock shows 100% cloud all day — assess KHAF METAR in morning to determine if coastal fog is at launch or below; if fog is at surface, site is shut
SST at Half Moon Bay buoy is 14.4°C — moderately cool, supporting a persistent marine layer influence on coastal sites through the week; burn-off timing may vary day to day
tldr: Today is a frustrating 'almost' day everywhere — a brief midday clearing window (roughly 12-1:30pm) at the inland sites gives Ed Levin and Mission Peak a slim marginal shot at weak thermals, while Mussel Rock's W-NW ridge flow doesn't arrive until after 2pm and stays on the light side. If you're going to fly today, hit Mussel Rock in the afternoon (2-6pm) or catch that narrow midday window at Ed Levin high launch with P3 patience. Monday and Tuesday look considerably better with clear skies and organized W-NW flow.
